nativeRange.habitat: Forest streams and small rivers with very clear, well-oxygenated water over substrates of rocks, boulders, and gravel covered in biofilm; marginal and overhanging vegetation is common, but aquatic macrophytes are sparse.

decorPreferences: dark substrate to intensify iridescent coloration
dense planting along sides and back
floating plants to diffuse surface light
smooth rocks or driftwood for visual cover

diet.notes: Primarily insectivorous in the wild; in captivity readily accepts quality micro-flake and small sinking pellets. Regular supplementation with small live or frozen foods (daphnia, brine shrimp nauplii, bloodworm, Artemia) improves condition and coloration.

avoidWith: slow-moving long-finned fish (fin-nipping risk in small groups)
large predatory fish that could swallow adults

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Lerdsuwa; 1400x933. Live in-tank Danio kerri showing the steel-blue lateral band and gold-orange stripe over a bluish body; only well-lit live CC photo of the whole fish available.

summary: A small, peaceful, iridescent schooling fish from the forest streams of southern Thailand and the Malay Peninsula. Generally considered cool-tolerant and undemanding, making it a popular choice for unheated community tanks. Best displayed in groups of 8 or more against a dark substrate.
careNotes:
The Blue Danio (Danio kerri) is one of the lesser-known Danionidae species, native to clear, fast-flowing forest streams in Phang Nga, Krabi, and Ranong provinces of southern Thailand, as well as islands including Phuket, Koh Yao Noi, Ko Lanta, and Langkawi, extending into the upper Malay Peninsula. Wild habitat is characterised by very clear, cool, well-oxygenated water over rocky substrates with biofilm but few aquatic macrophytes.

In the aquarium, a tank of at least 75 L with an 80 cm footprint is generally recommended, with good filtration providing 4–5× volume turnover to replicate the moderate current of its native streams. The species is notably cool-tolerant (20–26°C) and can be maintained in unheated setups in temperate climates, though sudden temperature swings should be avoided. Soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.5–7.5, gH 2–12) best reflects wild conditions.

A tight-fitting lid is essential — the Blue Danio is an accomplished jumper and escapes are common in open-top setups. Coloration, especially the characteristic steel-blue iridescence, is most vivid over dark substrates under subdued or diffused lighting. Groups of 8–10 or more are strongly recommended; smaller groups result in skittish behaviour, reduced coloration, and increased fin-nipping.

The Blue Danio is frequently confused with the Pearl Danio (Danio albolineatus), which shares a similar pale iridescent body plan and overlapping range. D. kerri tends toward a more uniform blue-steel sheen versus the pink-orange overtones and more distinct lateral stripe of D. albolineatus. The family Danionidae (including Danio, Devario, Microdevario, and related genera) was elevated from a subfamily of Cyprinidae to full family status on the basis of molecular phylogenetic studies, and current ichthyological literature now places it within Cypriniformes as family Danionidae.
breedingNotes: Egg scatterer with no parental care. Spawning is readily triggered in a mature, well-planted tank by conditioning the group on live or frozen foods and performing a small cool water change to mimic seasonal conditions. Eggs are deposited among fine-leaved plants or over a spawning mop and hatch in approximately 24–48 hours at 24°C. Adults will consume eggs and fry; removing the spawning group immediately after spawning or using a fine-mesh spawning grate substantially improves fry yield. Fry are free-swimming within a few days and accept infusoria, green water, or commercial fry foods initially, transitioning to micro-crushed flake and baby brine shrimp as they grow. Breeding is generally considered straightforward within the genus.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Danio kerri
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (40–45 mm SL), temperature (18–26°C), pH (6.5–7.5), hardness (36–215 ppm as CaCO3 ≈ 2–12 dGH), native range (southern Thailand: Phang Nga, Krabi, Ranong provinces and islands; upper Malay Peninsula including Langkawi), habitat (forest streams, rocky substrates, clear water, minimal macrophytes), minimum tank dimensions (80×30 cm), diet (insectivorous wild; accepts dry and frozen in captivity), behaviour (very peaceful, schooling, accomplished jumper, min group 8–10), and breeding notes (egg scatterer, no parental care). Temperature lower bound raised to 20°C per task brief (keeper-safe range); upper 26°C retained.

additional.name: FishBase — Danio kerri
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum adult size (5.0 cm TL male/unsexed), taxonomy (family Danionidae, order Cypriniformes), native range (Malay Peninsula), temperature (23–25°C optimal), pH (6.5–7.0), hardness (dH 5–12). IUCN status Least Concern (2011). Species named after Dr Arthur Francis George Kerr (1877–1942), Irish physician and botanist. FishBase now lists family as Danionidae (elevated from Cyprinidae subfamily per molecular phylogeny).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Zebra Danio Care (general danio husbandry)
additional.notes: No dedicated Blue Danio / Danio kerri page found on Aquarium Co-Op. General danio husbandry guide consulted for cross-check of feeding (flakes, nano pellets, frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp), temperature range (65–75°F / 18–24°C for danios broadly), fin-nipping risk in small groups (< 5–6 fish), and tank footprint guidance (20-gallon long recommended for active danios). Blue Danio-specific parameters taken from SeriouslyFish and FishBase.

additional.name: Pearl Danio file cross-reference — Danio albolineatus
additional.notes: Consulted to document the Blue Danio / Pearl Danio confusion issue and confirm distinguishing features. D. albolineatus shares the iridescent pale-body appearance and overlapping Southeast Asian range; differs by pink-orange overtones and more prominent lateral stripe versus the blue-steel uniform sheen of D. kerri. Both are Danionidae, Cypriniformes.

fish.breedingNotes: The Blue Danio is an egg scatterer with no parental care. Condition the group on live or frozen foods, then trigger spawning with a small, slightly cooler water change. Remove the adults immediately after spawning to protect the eggs. Eggs hatch in roughly 24 to 48 hours at 24°C, and fry accept infusoria before transitioning to micro-crushed flake and baby brine shrimp.
